title: Chapter 13: The Other Woman
content: I should tell you about the other woman. Not Vivienne. The other other woman. The one Tom Hargrove keeps in the Valley like a shameful secret, the one who doesn't get hologram parties or Schiaparelli dresses, the one who exists in the margins of a story that was never about her.
Her name is Cassandra.

The dry cleaner's press thumps through the floor like a second heartbeat—chung, chung, chung—even at this hour. Old Mr. Pak works late. He always works late. Cassandra Rivera has learned to sleep through it, the way you learn to sleep through anything when the alternative is not sleeping at all.

She sits cross-legged on Egyptian cotton sheets that cost more than her mother made in a month. Tom's taste. Everything in this apartment is Tom's taste: the espresso machine she doesn't use, the Eames chair she's afraid to sit in, the art on the walls—abstract, expensive, meaningless. The apartment is a set dressing, and she is the prop arranged just so.

One bedroom above a dry cleaner in Canoga Park. That's what five hundred thousand dollars of a venture capitalist's guilt buys you. Not a view. Not a neighborhood. A one-bedroom with nice sheets and thin walls where you can hear steam hissing through pipes at midnight and the muffled thump of someone else's labor beneath your feet.
Cassandra scrolls her phone.

Jax Vance's name is everywhere. The Memory party—she'd seen the invites circulated, the location teased, the gossip aggregated across every feed that matters and most that don't. Now it's all aftermath. The woman in white. A hologram dance. And then: the kiss.

The photo is everywhere. A woman in a white dress, leaning into another woman under a canopy of projected stars. The image is slightly blurred, caught mid-motion, which makes it look more intimate than if it were sharp. The headline beneath it reads: *Vivienne Sterling-Hargrove Spotted in Rare PDA at Vance Bash*.
Cassandra's stomach turns.

Not jealousy. She needs that to be clear, even if no one is asking. Not jealousy. She stopped being jealous of Vivienne a long time ago, around the time she realized that being the woman on the pedestal and being the woman under it were just two different ways of being crushed.

Recognition. That's what coils in her gut, sour and immediate. She knows what it means to be Tom's secret. She knows what it means to be the name he never says in certain company, the face he never posts, the obligation he fulfills with cash left on a nightstand like a tip after a service rendered.
The press thumps below. Chung. Chung. Chung.
She locks her phone and sets it face-down on the Egyptian cotton, and she waits.
He comes at ten.

He always comes at ten, and he always stays forty-five minutes, and the regularity of it is the worst part. Not the brevity. The precision. Tom Hargrove runs on a schedule even when he's slumming it in the Valley, even when he's visiting the woman he fucks but doesn't kiss, and that schedule tells Cassandra everything she needs to know about where she ranks.
At 9:57, she hears the car. Not a garage—there's no garage here—but the throaty idle of a Mercedes pulling to the curb outside. The engine cuts. A door shuts. Footsteps on the exterior stairs.
At 10:00, his key in the lock.

"Hey." He says it casually, already loosening his tie. He's still in his suit from whatever dinner or meeting or networking event filled his evening, and he looks good. He always looks good. That's part of the problem. Tom Hargrove is a handsome man with a warm voice and easy charm, and if you didn't know better—if you only saw him across a room, working a crowd—you'd think he was kind.
Cassandra knows better.

"Hey." She's on the couch, knees pulled up. She doesn't get up to greet him anymore. She used to. She used to meet him at the door with a drink and a smile, playing the part of the woman who was happy to see him. That was eighteen months ago. She was twenty-seven then, and she still thought this arrangement had a ceiling that might lift.

Tonight he's agitated. She can see it in the way he moves—too quick, too precise, like a man working to keep his hands steady. He tosses his tie over the back of the Eames chair, unbuttons his collar, and goes straight for the whisky on the kitchen counter. Not the good stuff Tom bought. The cheap stuff Cassandra bought. He pours two fingers and drinks it standing up.
"Long night?" she asks.
He grunts. Then, sideways, without looking at her: "My wife has a new friend."

Four words that open a door, but Cassandra doesn't walk through it. She's learned. Push, and the charm curdles. Ask a question he doesn't want to answer, and the warmth in his voice goes brittle, like frost on glass. He doesn't get angry—not ever, not loudly. He just gets cold, and the cold is worse.
"Okay," she says.

She gets up and goes to him. Takes the glass from his hand. Pours the remainder down the sink and replaces it with the good whisky from the bottle he brought last month. The label is in French. She can't read it. It burns the same.
He downs it in one swallow.

"Vivienne was at a party tonight," he says, as if Cassandra hasn't already seen the photos, as if she doesn't already know. "With some woman. Public." He says *public* like it's a disease. "She was *kissing* her. Someone got a photo."
"I saw."
His eyes snap to her. A flash of something—surprise? Anger?—before the mask slides back. "You saw."
"It's on every feed, Tom."

He doesn't like that. She can tell by the way his jaw sets, the slight pull at the corner of his mouth. He doesn't like that his secret woman is watching his public wife. The overlap makes him uncomfortable. The categories are supposed to stay separate.
He sets the empty glass on the counter. Buttons his shirt—two buttons, three, four—retrieves his tie from the chair. He's getting ready to leave, and he's only been here nineteen minutes.
Cassandra doesn't comment. Nineteen is a new record.

He pulls his wallet from his jacket and sets five hundred-dollar bills on the nightstand. The bills land soft, soundless, on the Egyptian cotton. He doesn't fold them. Doesn't tuck them under anything. Just sets them there, visible, like a receipt.
He doesn't kiss her goodbye.
He hasn't in months.

"Bolts at ten forty-three on a Monday," she might say to someone, if there were anyone to say it to. "Leaves five hundred on the nightstand like he's settling a tab. Doesn't lock the door on the way out because the lock is his, the door is his, the sheets are his, and I am his."
But there's no one to say it to. So she just listens to the Mercedes start up outside, and the engine fade, and the press thump back into the silence below.
Chung. Chung. Chung.

The Bleu de Chanel lingers after he leaves. It hangs in the apartment's stale air, mixing with the chemical sweetness of dry-cleaning solvent that vents up through the floorboards. Cassandra sits on the edge of the bed—*his* bed, always his—and breathes it in. Not because she wants to. Because she can't not.
She picks up her phone. Opens a browser. Types: *Vivienne Sterling-Hargrove*.

The results are predictable and voluminous. Society pages. Charity galas. A profile in Los Angeles Magazine: "The Quiet Power Behind Hargrove Ventures." Photos at the Getty, at the Disney Concert Hall, at benefits for children's hospitals and literacy foundations and clean water initiatives. Vivienne in Valentino. Vivienne in Dior. Vivienne with her hair swept up, diamonds at her ears, smile Camera-ready, practiced, *perfect*.
One image stops Cassandra's thumb.

Vivienne at a gala—last autumn, the Children's Defense Fund dinner. Diamond earrings that catch the photographer's flash like tiny suns. A fitted black gown. And Tom's hand on her waist, proprietary and relaxed, the way you'd rest your hand on something you own.
Both smiling. Both hollow.

Cassandra sees it in the way Vivienne's eyes don't quite match her mouth. The smile is all lips and teeth, but the eyes are somewhere else—somewhere flat and distant, like a lake in winter. She's seen that look before. In her own mirror.
It hits her like a math equation that suddenly solves itself, all the variables clicking into place at once: She and Vivienne are mirrors. One displayed, one hidden. Both owned.

Vivienne gets the galas and the diamonds and the public hand on the waist. Cassandra gets the apartment above the dry cleaner and the five hundred dollars on the nightstand. But the architecture is identical. A man who keeps what he wants and discards the rest. A man who arranges women into categories—displayed, hidden, useful, disposable—and moves between them without ever thinking about what they might want, because their wanting has never been part of the equation.
The gala photo stays on her screen. Vivienne's flat eyes stare out at her.
*You too*, Cassandra thinks. *You too.*
She switches the phone off. Sets it down. The press thumps below. The cologne fades. The apartment settles into its usual silence—expensive, empty, paid for.
She drives home.

Home is a duplex in Panorama City with a driveway that cracks every summer and a kitchen where the linoleom curls at the edges. Home smells like motor oil and coffee and the particular staleness of rooms where the windows haven't been opened in weeks. Home is where Mateo is.
He's awake when she walks in.

He's sitting at the kitchen table, surrounded by bills. They're fanned out in front of him like a hand of cards he was dealt and can't fold fast enough—red final-notice stamps, past-due balances, a letter from the landlord that's too thick to be anything but a warning. The garage is failing. She knows this. Rivera Auto has been running in the red since the transmission lift broke in March and Mateo couldn't afford to replace it. Three months behind on rent. Behind on the distributor. Behind on everything.

The kitchen light is fluorescent and unforgiving. It carves shadows under Mateo's eyes and turns his skin sallow. His hands are flat on the table, fingers spread, and even from across the room Cassandra can smell the motor oil—ground into his cuticles, settled in the creases of his knuckles, permanent as a tattoo. He's been working late. He always works late.
"Hey," she says.
"Hey." He doesn't look up from the bills.

She pads to the counter in bare feet—the shoes she wore to the apartment are in her bag, she changed into sandals for the drive—and the kitchen tile is cold. Cold and gritty with the dirt that nothing keeps out because the seal on the back door is shot and the landlord won't fix it, same landlord whose thick letter sits in front of Mateo like a verdict.

She opens the cabinet above the sink. The cheap whisky—same bottle Tom drained a finger from hours ago and a freeway away—sits between a half-empty bag of rice and a jar of instant coffee. She pours two inches into a glass that's not clean, drinks it. It burns. Sharp and chemical, nothing like the French stuff, and the burn is almost a relief. It's *real*. Everything in Tom's apartment is smooth—smooth sheets, smooth walls, smooth lies. This is rough. This is hers.
"Where do you go at night?"
Mateo's voice is quiet. Not angry. Not accusatory. Just quiet, the way a room is quiet right before you notice it's filling with water.
Cassandra freezes with the glass halfway to her lips. Sets it down. Wipes her mouth with the back of her hand.
"For walks," she says.

The silence that follows is enormous. It fills the kitchen, pressing against the curling linoleom and the fluorescent light and the bills on the table. It's louder than a fight. A fight would be something—a spark, a collision, energy discharged. This is the absence of discharge. This is the question that was asked and the answer that was a lie and the knowledge they both carry that the lie was told and heard and accepted anyway, because the alternative is opening a door neither of them is ready to walk through.

Mateo looks at her. His eyes are dark and tired and he smells like oil and his hands are still flat on the bills—hands that fixed a thousand cars for a thousand people who never thought about the man beneath the hood, who never asked if he was okay, who just wanted their engines to start.
He doesn't push.
She doesn't explain.

The refrigerator hums. The fluorescent light buzzes. Somewhere outside, a dog barks once and stops. Cassandra stands at the counter in bare feet on cold tile, and Mateo sits at the table with the bills arranged around him like an ambush, and the distance between them is six feet of kitchen and a lie she can't take back and a truth she can't speak.
She picks up the glass and drinks again. The burn is less this time. That's the problem with cheap whisky: you adapt.
That's the problem with everything.
Cassandra Rivera didn't know it yet, but she was about to become the most dangerous person in this story.
Not because she had power. Because she had nothing left to lose.

Mateo was suspicious, and suspicious men ask questions they can't un-ask. Tom was careless, and careless men leave trails they can't un-leave. And Cassandra—Cassandra was standing at the exact intersection of knowing and being known, the place where secrets go to die.

Here's what I know that she doesn't: secrets only stay secret when everyone involved has something to protect. Cassandra is about to run out of things. And a woman with nothing to protect is a woman who will burn everything down just to see the light.
I should know.
I'm writing this one.
